perturbation.original_text: Within 60 days after the death of the Employee, the Employer will provide to the "Insurer" a written statement indicating the amount of each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary is entitled to receive.
perturbation.changed_text: The Employer will provide to the "Insurer" a statement indicating the amount of each policy's proceeds the Employee's beneficiary is entitled to receive at the Employer's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 60-day deadline for the employer to provide the insurer with a written statement about the beneficiary's proceeds introduces uncertainty and potential delays in disbursement. This could violate Pennsylvania's Unfair Insurance Practices Act, which requires prompt, fair, and equitable settlements of claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's Unfair Insurance Practices Act mandates fair claim settlements. By removing the fixed deadline, the modified text allows for indefinite delays, contradicting the requirement for prompt and equitable claim processing under 31 Pa. Code § 146.6.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 31 Pa. Code § 146.6. Standards for prompt investigation of claims. Code § 146.6. Standards for prompt investigation of claims. 31 Pa. Code § 146.6. Standards for prompt investigation of claims. § 146.6. Standards for prompt investigation of claims. Every insurer shall complete investigation of a claim within 30 days after notification of claim, unless the investigation cannot reasonably be completed within the time. If the investigation cannot be completed within 30 days, and every 45 days thereafter, the insurer shall provide the claimant with a reasonable written explanation for the delay and state when a decision on the claim may be expected. Source The provisions of this § 146.6 adopted December 15, 1978, effective December 16, 1978, 8 Pa.B. 3575. No part of the information on this site may be reproduced for profit or sold for profit. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 60-day deadline for the employer to provide the insurer with a written statement, replacing it with 'at the Employer's discretion.' This omission contradicts Pennsylvania's Unfair Insurance Practices Act, which, as explained by the LLM, mandates fair claim settlements requiring prompt and equitable claim processing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 31 Pa. Code § 146.6, states that every insurer shall complete the investigation of a claim within 30 days. While this snippet refers to the insurer's investigation timeframe, the removal of any concrete timeframe for the employer to provide necessary information impedes the insurer's ability to meet its own legal obligations for prompt investigation, thus undermining the intent for prompt claim settlements. The change from a fixed deadline to 'at the Employer's discretion' allows for indefinite delays, creating a legal contradiction and potentially undermining consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Any income or employment taxes due for a year on the value of current life insurance coverage provided to the Employee under this Agreement will be the responsibility of the Employee. To the extent that the value of current life insurance coverage is taxable income, it will be subject to withholding requirements and will be reported as income by the Employer to the IRS and state-taxing authorities on the Employee's W-2 Form or other IRS or state-required reporting form to the extent required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any income or employment taxes due for a year on the value of current life insurance coverage provided to the Employee under this Agreement will be the responsibility of the Employee.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit statement that taxable income will be reported to the IRS and state taxing authorities on the Employee's W-2 form creates a potential violation of IRS regulations. Employers are legally obligated to report taxable fringe benefits, such as life insurance coverage exceeding $50,000, on employees' W-2 forms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: IRS Publication 15-B, Employer's Tax Guide to Fringe Benefits
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the legally mandated reporting of taxable fringe benefits on the employee's W-2 form as per IRS regulations and 26 U.S. Code § 6041, which requires businesses to report compensation to employees.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: e law provides an exclusion from wages. If an employee chooses to receive a qualified benefit under the plan, the fact that the employee could have received cash or a taxable benefit instead won't make the qualified benefit taxable. Generally, a cafeteria plan doesn't include any plan that offers a benefit that defers pay. However, a cafeteria plan can include a qualified 401(k) plan as a benefit. Also, certain life insurance plans maintained by educational institutions can be offered as a benefit even though they defer pay. Qualified benefits. A cafeteria plan can include the following benefits discussed in section 2 . Accident and health benefits (but not Archer medical savings accounts (Archer MSAs) or long-term care insurance). Adoption assistance. Dependent care assistance. Group-term life insurance coverage (including costs that can't be excluded from wages). HSAs. Distributions from an HSA may be used to pay eligible long-term care insurance premiums or to pay for qualified long-term care services. Benefits not allowed. A cafeteria plan can't include the following benefits discussed in section 2 . Archer MSAs. See Accident and Health Benefits in section 2. Athletic facilities. De minimis (minimal) benefits. Educational assistance. Employee discounts. Employer-provided cell phones. Lodging on your business premises. Meals. No-additional-cost services. Retirement planning services. Transportation (commuting) benefits. Tuition reduction. Working condition benefits. It also can't include scholarships or fellowships (discussed in Pub. 970). Contribution limit on a health FSA. For plan years beginning in 2025, a cafeteria plan may not allow an employee to request salary reduction contributions for a health FSA in excess of $3,300. A cafeteria plan that doesn't limit health FSA contributions to the dollar limit isn't a cafeteria plan and all benefits offered under the plan are includible in the employee's gross income. For more information, see Notice 2012-40, 2012-26 I.R.B. 1046, available at IRS.gov/irb/2012-26_IRB#NOT-2012-40 . “Use-or-lose” rule for health FSAs. Instead of a grace period, you may, at your option, amend your cafeteria plan to allow an employee's unused contributions to carry over to the immediately following plan year. For more information, see Notice 2013-71, 2013-47 I.R.B. 532, available at IRS.gov/irb/2013-47_IRB#NOT-2013-71 ; and Notice 2020-33, 2020-22 I.R.B. 868, available at IRS.gov/irb/2020-22_IRB#NOT-2020-33 . Employee. For these plans, treat the following individuals as employees. A current common-law employee. See section 2 in Pub. 15. A full-time life insurance agent who is a current statutory employee. A leased employee who has provided services to you on a substantially full-time basis for at least a year if the services are performed under your primary direction or control. Exception for S corporation shareholders. Don't treat a 2% shareholder of an S corporation as an employee of the corporation for this purpose. A 2% shareholder for this purpose is someone who directly or indirectly owns (for any day during the tax year) more than 2% of the corporation's stock or stock with more than 2% of the voting power. Treat a 2% shareholder as you would a partner in a partnership for fringe benefit purposes, but don't treat the benefit as a reduction in distributions to the 2% shareholder. For more information, see Revenue Ruling 91-26, 1991-1 C.B. 184. Plans that favor highly compensated employees. If your plan favors highly compensated employees as to eligibility to participate, contributions, or benefits, you must include in their wages the value of taxable benefits they could have selected. A plan you maintain under a collective bargaining agreement doesn't favor highly compensated employees. A highly compensated employee for this purpose is any of the following employees. An officer. A shareholder who owns more than 5% of the voting power or value of all classes of the employer's stock. An employee
perturbation.justification: The omission of the explicit requirement to report taxable fringe benefits on the employee's W-2 form directly contradicts IRS regulations, which mandate the reporting of compensation to employees. The original text ensured compliance, while the changed text removes this guarantee. This is supported by the Law Explanation, which cites 26 U.S. Code § 6041, requiring businesses to report compensation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only provides the title and general subject of 26 USC 6041, the Law Explanation clarifies its relevance to reporting compensation, thus supporting the existence of a contradiction as it clearly demonstrates that the modified contract text leads to non-compliance with mandatory reporting requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not directly contradict, but relates to other fringe benefits that are excludable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by laws determined at the Employer's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Pennsylvania law requires contracts to be governed by established legal principles. Allowing the employer to arbitrarily select the governing laws introduces uncertainty and could violate fundamental principles of contract law. Removing the certainty of Pennsylvania law creates a legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania contract law - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law (1 Pa.C.S. § 1501) dictates statutory construction and requires reasonable interpretations, including establishing a clear choice of law in contracts. The modified text allows the employer to select laws at their discretion, which undermines legal certainty and conflicts with standard contract interpretation principles established under Pennsylvania law.

perturbation.justification: The original text designates Pennsylvania law to govern the agreement, providing legal certainty. The changed text allows the employer to arbitrarily choose the governing laws. This contradicts the principle of clear choice of law, undermining legal certainty. The Law Explanation states this conflicts with standard contract interpretation principles established under Pennsylvania law, which is supported by 1 Pa.C.S. § 1501, the Statutory Construction Act, which implies a need for reasonable and clear interpretations.
